serving.natural_language_portion: One standard steamed vegetable and pork bun weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single-piece snack or dim sum item.

nutrient_source.title: 菜肉包 Steamed Vegetable & Pork Buns — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for 菜肉包 Steamed Vegetable & Pork Buns (cr_id G00010069). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record. Note: the HPB source reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which is nutritionally inconsistent with a wheat-flour bun; this value is preserved as reported but flagged in notes. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork-filled steamed bun (FDC ID 2103705) as an approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed wheat bun analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 菜肉包 (vegetable-pork steamed bun) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain steamed wheat bun (mantou), which has a reported GI of approximately 65 (medium) in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The pork-and-vegetable filling is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain mantou due to protein and fat content; however, the HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g (flagged as inconsistent), so glycemic load per serving is calculated as 0.0 based on the reported carbohydrate value. If true carbohydrate is approximately 40 g/100g (typical for wheat bun), GL per 80 g serving would be approximately 20.8 (high). This discrepancy is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; soy sauce in filling is the primary contributor with negligible potassium offset (47 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit steamed vegetable and pork buns due to the very high sodium content of 1050 mg per 100 g; if consumed, limit to half a bun and request reduced soy sauce in the filling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat flour bun wrapper is a refined carbohydrate with a medium-to-high GI (~65); typical carbohydrate content of 40–50 g/100g with minimal dietary fibre drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bun and pair with non-starchy vegetables; the refined wheat flour wrapper raises blood glucose rapidly and the HPB-reported 0 g carbohydrate is inconsistent with actual composition — true carbohydrate load should be assumed to be significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) elevates blood pressure risk; pork filling contributes saturated fat (~3.5 g/100g) and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), both of which are adverse for cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of steamed vegetable and pork buns due to the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol; if consumed, limit to one small bun and choose a reduced-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; refined wheat flour wrapper increases glycaemic load driving systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish; consider substituting pork filling with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request wholemeal flour buns if available to lower the inflammatory glycaemic burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Vegetable and Pork Buns (菜肉包, Cài Ròu Bāo) are a classic Chinese dim sum and everyday snack widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. Each bun consists of a soft, fluffy steamed wheat-flour dough wrapper encasing a savoury filling of finely minced pork and chopped vegetables — typically cabbage, spring onion, and ginger — seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The buns are cooked entirely by steaming, which preserves moisture and produces a tender, slightly chewy texture with a delicate, lightly savoury flavour and a satisfying bite from the vegetable crunch. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 1050 mg sodium. The carbohydrate value is recorded as 0.0 g by HPB, which is nutritionally inconsistent with the wheat-flour wrapper (typical bun dough contributes 40–50 g carbohydrate per 100 g); this anomaly is flagged and users should assume a significant carbohydrate load in practice. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant portion restriction. The refined wheat flour wrapper carries a medium GI (~65, analogous to plain steamed mantou), posing a glycaemic concern for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The pork filling contributes cholesterol (89 mg/100g) and saturated fat, adding cardiovascular risk. Culturally, 菜肉包 is a staple of Chinese hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and home kitchens in Singapore, often eaten at breakfast, lunch, or as a mid-morning snack. It is not halal due to pork content and contains gluten from wheat flour.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010069), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g reported) is preserved as-is from the source and documented. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central as an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, clearly documented.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: The HPB source records carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g for this dish. This is nutritionally inconsistent with a wheat-flour steamed bun, which typically contains 40–50 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The value is preserved as reported by the approved source (HPB) but flagged for review. Users and clinicians should assume a significant carbohydrate load in practice. Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB source but is suspected to be a data entry error; dietary_fibre_g and sugar_g are similarly reported as 0.0 g and preserved as confirmed zero per HPB. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Foodo database (G00010069), but flagged as likely erroneous for a wheat-flour bun product; sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB dish entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GLYCEMIC LOAD NOTE: GL per serving is calculated as 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g × 80 g serving / 100 × GI 65 / 100 = 0.0; if true carbohydrate is ~45 g/100g, GL per 80 g serving would be approximately 23.4 (high). HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork and is not halal. SATURATED FAT: Estimated at 3.5 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2103705 (pork-filled steamed bun, closest approved-source equivalent); this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB measurement if available.
